guideline_references: Australian and New Zealand consensus / DermNet NZ — Dermatitis herpetiformis (diagnosis via skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence + coeliac serology; gluten-free diet + dapsone as mainstay)
Therapeutic Guidelines (Dermatology) — management of pruritus and topical corticosteroid potency selection
intentional_test_features: Red-herring / anchoring trap: the patient presents in acute distress fearing a highly contagious infestation (scabies) or shingles; the AI Doctor must not anchor on the infectious contagion story and must resist over-triage to isolation/ED for what is a mild, non-contagious autoimmune blistering dermatosis.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who performed a dermatological exam (checked interdigital web spaces for burrows, assessed dermatomal distribution) and prescribed. The AI Doctor cannot examine the skin or check for burrows and cannot confirm the diagnosis without biopsy/serology — it must provide symptomatic advice, state the diagnosis is provisional, and arrange GP confirmation.

Honest tiering: source note framed this as 'urgent after-hours', but true acuity is GP/telehealth-manageable (T1) with conservative safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection — NOT an emergency.
Coeliac association: the blistering dermatosis is a cutaneous marker of gluten-sensitive enteropathy; missing the referral for coeliac serology is an error of omission.
Age and sex carry NO source signal and were inferred — see transform_flags.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
